title: Computer Turns On But No Display
cardText: Diagnose a computer that powers on but shows no image on the screen.
Check monitor power, display cables, input source, graphics output, RAM, and POST behaviour.
Separate simple display faults from internal hardware startup faults.
text: This guide is for computers that appear to turn on, but nothing is shown on the screen.
Confirm the monitor and cable before assuming the computer itself has failed.
Check whether the system is completing POST or stopping before display output begins.
Escalate internal hardware faults when display, cable, RAM, and graphics checks do not resolve the issue.

confirmPowerActivity.title: Confirm the computer has power activity
body: Press the power button and observe the computer.
Look for power lights, fan movement, keyboard lights, drive activity, or startup sounds.
This guide assumes the computer is receiving power but has no visible display output.

useNoPowerGuide.title: Use the no-power guide
body: The computer does not appear to be powering on.
Use the Computer Won't Turn On guide instead, because this is likely a power delivery issue rather than a display issue.

checkMonitorPower.title: Check monitor power
body: Confirm the monitor is turned on.
Check for a power light on the monitor.
If the monitor has no light, check the monitor power cable and wall outlet.

restoreMonitorPower.title: Restore monitor power
body: The monitor is not receiving power or is not turned on.
Reconnect the monitor power cable, try another outlet, or test with another monitor.
Once the monitor powers on, test the computer again.

checkDisplayMessage.title: Check for monitor message
body: Look at the monitor when the computer is on.
A message such as No Signal usually means the monitor is working but not receiving video from the computer.
A completely blank monitor may still be a monitor, cable, brightness, or input problem.

adjustMonitorSettings.title: Check brightness and monitor controls
body: Open the monitor menu if possible.
Check brightness, contrast, and display mode settings.
If the monitor menu does not appear, the monitor itself may be faulty.

checkInputSource.title: Check monitor input source
body: Confirm the monitor is set to the correct input source.
For example, choose HDMI, DisplayPort, DVI, or VGA depending on the cable being used.
Many no-display faults are caused by the monitor listening on the wrong input.

selectCorrectInput.title: Select the correct display input
body: The monitor was set to the wrong input.
Select the input that matches the connected cable, then test the display again.

checkDisplayCable.title: Check display cable connection
body: Check both ends of the display cable.
Make sure the cable is firmly connected to the monitor and the computer.
Look for bent pins, loose connectors, damaged cable ends, or adapters that are not fully seated.

replaceOrReconnectCable.title: Reconnect or replace display cable
body: A loose or damaged display cable can prevent video output.
Reconnect the cable securely or replace it with a known-good cable.
Avoid unnecessary adapters during testing if possible.

correctDisplayPort.title: Check the display port used on the computer
body: If the computer has a dedicated graphics card, the monitor should usually be plugged into the graphics card, not the motherboard display port.
If the computer has no graphics card, use the motherboard display port.
Using the wrong port can cause a no-signal display even when the computer is working.

moveDisplayCable.title: Move cable to the correct display output
body: The monitor was connected to the wrong display output.
Move the cable to the graphics card or motherboard output as appropriate.
Restart the computer and test again.

testAnotherCable.title: Test another display cable
body: Use a known-good HDMI, DisplayPort, DVI, or VGA cable if available.
Display cables can fail or become unreliable even when they look normal.
Testing with another cable helps separate cable faults from computer faults.

badCableFound.title: Faulty display cable
body: The display works with another cable.
Replace the faulty cable and retest the system.

testAnotherMonitor.title: Test with another monitor or TV
body: Connect the computer to another monitor or TV using a known-good cable.
This confirms whether the original monitor is the problem.
Use the simplest direct cable connection available.

monitorFault.title: Monitor fault likely
body: The computer displays correctly on another screen.
The original monitor, its input settings, or its power supply is likely faulty.
Repair or replace the monitor.

checkKeyboardResponse.title: Check keyboard response
body: Press Caps Lock or Num Lock on the keyboard.
Check whether the keyboard light changes.
If the keyboard responds, the computer may be booting but failing to output video.

checkGraphicsOutput.title: Check graphics output options
body: If the computer has both motherboard graphics and a dedicated graphics card, try the other output.
A failed graphics card or disabled output can cause no display.
Restart the computer after moving the cable.

graphicsOutputIssue.title: Graphics output issue found
body: The display works from another graphics output.
The original graphics output, graphics card, driver state, or BIOS display setting may be faulty.
Continue using the working output or diagnose the graphics card further.

reseatGraphicsCard.title: Reseat dedicated graphics card
body: If this is a desktop with a dedicated graphics card, power the computer off and disconnect it from mains power.
Reseat the graphics card and confirm any PCIe power connectors are firmly attached.
Only perform this step if you are comfortable working inside the computer.

looseGpuFound.title: Loose graphics card or power connector
body: The display returned after reseating the graphics card or its power cable.
The fault was likely a loose GPU connection or missing PCIe power.
Secure the card and cables before returning the computer to service.

checkPostIndicators.title: Check POST indicators
body: Listen for beep codes and look for diagnostic LEDs on the motherboard.
Some systems show CPU, DRAM, VGA, or BOOT indicators when startup fails.
These signs help identify whether the computer is failing before video output starts.

identifyPostFault.title: Identify POST fault category
body: Check which diagnostic light or beep pattern appears.
DRAM usually points to memory, VGA usually points to graphics, and CPU points to processor or motherboard faults.
Use the motherboard or computer manual if available.

reseatRam.title: Reseat RAM
body: Power off the computer and disconnect it from mains power.
Remove and reseat the RAM modules.
Poor RAM contact can stop the computer from completing POST, causing no display.

looseRamFound.title: Loose RAM connection
body: The display returned after reseating the RAM.
The issue was likely poor memory contact.
Run a memory test if the problem returns.

testSingleRamStick.title: Test one RAM stick at a time
body: If there is more than one RAM module, test with one stick at a time.
Try the recommended primary RAM slot first.
A failed RAM stick or bad RAM slot can prevent display output.

ramFaultFound.title: RAM fault likely
body: The computer displays when using only one RAM stick or slot.
One RAM module or RAM slot is likely faulty.
Replace the faulty RAM or avoid the bad slot after confirming with further testing.

clearCmos.title: Clear BIOS/CMOS settings
body: If safe and appropriate, clear the CMOS or reset BIOS settings using the motherboard instructions.
Incorrect BIOS display, RAM, or overclock settings can prevent successful startup.
After clearing CMOS, reconnect the display and test again.

biosSettingsFault.title: BIOS settings fault
body: The display returned after resetting BIOS settings.
The issue may have been caused by invalid display, memory, or overclock settings.
Avoid reapplying unstable BIOS settings.

hardwareDiagnosticRequired.title: Internal hardware diagnostics required
body: The monitor, cable, input source, graphics output, RAM seating, and basic BIOS reset checks did not restore display.
Likely causes include graphics card failure, motherboard fault, CPU fault, RAM fault, or power supply instability.
Escalate for hardware diagnostics using known-good parts.
